reasoning: The permission_callback at lines 102-113 of class-omnisend-core-bootstrap.php returns is_user_logged_in(), confirming any authenticated user passes the REST gate. However, the callback omnisend_post_connection() (class-connection.php line 221) immediately checks current_user_can('manage_options') and returns a 200 JSON error if it fails — before nonce validation (line 230), before any Omnisend API call, and before any state mutation. A subscriber or customer receives only a static error string ('You do not sufficient permissions perform action.') with HTTP 200. No sensitive data leaks, no API consumption, no enumeration surface beyond a hardcoded string. The nonce ('connect') is generated on the admin settings page which itself requires manage_options, so non-admins cannot obtain a valid nonce anyway. The claimed impact (nonce-validation logic triggered, API calls consumed, enumeration-useful error details) is factually incorrect — execution terminates at the inner capability check. The architectural defect (wrong gate in wrong layer) is real as a code-quality issue, but there is no exploitable vulnerability because the defense-in-depth inside the callback fully neutralises the weak outer gate.
